findings.location: encrypted-key.tpl.xml.js line 6 — template literal interpolation of `keyInfo`
findings.reachability: Any caller of the `encrypt` function that controls `options.pem` or intermediate keyInfo rendering; library users constructing encryption options.
findings.justification: The `keyInfo` variable is interpolated directly into the XML template on line 6 (`${keyInfo}`) without any escaping. `keyInfo` is itself rendered by `keyinfo.tpl.xml.js`, which embeds `${encryptionPublicCert}` raw on its line 10. `encryptionPublicCert` is built in xmlenc.js line 29 by string-concatenating raw XML around `pemToCert(options.pem.toString())`. If `options.pem` contains XML-significant characters in the cert body position (e.g., via a crafted PEM), they are inserted into the output document unescaped, enabling XML structure manipulation.

detection_pattern.section_name: XML injection: unescaped template interpolation in XML output
detection_pattern.description: Finds template literal interpolations inside XML-generating JS template files. When the interpolated variable is not wrapped in an escaping function (escape-html, xmlEscape, etc.), attacker-controlled input can break out of the XML text context.

findings.reachability: Same as above — caller controls `options.pem`, whose parsed form is placed into `encryptionPublicCert` and then interpolated unescaped into a KeyInfo XML block.
findings.justification: Inside `keyinfo.tpl.xml.js`, the `encryptionPublicCert` parameter is interpolated without escaping on line 10. It is constructed in xmlenc.js as the literal string `'<X509Data><X509Certificate>' + utils.pemToCert(options.pem.toString()) + '</X509Certificate></X509Data>'`. Although the base64 content of a well-formed cert is safe, a crafted or truncated PEM could produce output that closes the `<X509Certificate>` tag early and injects sibling elements into the XML KeyInfo block, affecting downstream XML signature or decryption logic.

detection_pattern.section_name: XML injection: unescaped certificate data in XML template
detection_pattern.description: Template literal interpolations in XML output files without escaping allow cert or key material strings to inject XML structure if the input deviates from the expected base64 format.

findings.location: utils.js `pemToCert` function, line 15 — `cert[1]` accessed without null check
findings.reachability: Any caller passing a non-PEM or malformed string as `options.pem` to `encryptKeyInfo`; reachable from the public `encrypt` API.
findings.justification: In `pemToCert`, the regex `/-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----([^-]*)-----END CERTIFICATE-----/g.exec(pem)` returns `null` when `pem` does not match. The code immediately accesses `cert[1]` and `cert.length` on the next line without a null guard, causing an uncaught `TypeError: Cannot read properties of null` that crashes the Node.js process. This is a denial-of-service vector for any service that passes caller-supplied PEM data.

detection_pattern.section_name: Null dereference: regex exec result used without null check
detection_pattern.description: Identifies `.exec()` calls whose return value is used in the next statement without a null check. `RegExp.prototype.exec` returns null on no-match; accessing properties on null causes a TypeError crash.

findings.reachability: Any concurrent invocation of `encrypt` in a multi-request Node.js server environment; no external input needed beyond concurrent use.
findings.justification: Inside `encryptKeyInfoWithScheme`, the variables `base64EncodedEncryptedKey`, `params`, and `result` are assigned without `var`, `let`, or `const`, making them implicit globals on the module's global object. Under concurrent async calls, a second in-flight encryption can overwrite `params` or `result` before the first call reads them, causing key material (`encryptedKey`, `symmetricKey`) from one session to bleed into another session's output — a potentially critical confidentiality and integrity failure in high-concurrency scenarios.

detection_pattern.section_name: Implicit global variable: undeclared assignment in function scope
detection_pattern.description: Finds variable assignments inside functions that lack a `var`/`let`/`const` declaration. These become implicit globals in non-strict mode, creating shared mutable state that is unsafe under concurrency and leaks data between calls.
